formulas.name: Mean (Arithmetic Mean)
formulas.notes: To find the mean, sum all individual values and divide by the total number of observations.
formulas.units: depends on the units of xi
formulas.variables: xi: individual values, n: number of observations

formulas.name: Median
formulas.notes: For an even number of observations, take the average of the two middle values.
formulas.formula: Arrange data in ascending order, find the middle value if n is odd or average of two middle values if n is even
formulas.variables: xi: individual values, n: number of observations

formulas.name: Mode
formulas.notes: A set of data may have no mode, one mode, or multiple modes.

formulas.name: Range
formulas.notes: Range measures the spread of data.

formulas.name: Empirical Relation: Mean-Median-Mode
formulas.notes: If the distribution is well-behaved, the mean, median, and mode will be equal.
formulas.formula: For a well-behaved distribution, Mean = Median = Mode

formulas.name: Empirical Relation: Five-Number Summary
formulas.notes: The five-number summary provides a quick overview of a dataset's distribution.
formulas.formula: Minimum value, First quartile (Q1), Median (Q2), Third quartile (Q3), Maximum value
formulas.variables: Minimum value, First quartile (Q1), Median (Q2), Third quartile (Q3), Maximum value

formulas.name: Law of Large Numbers (LLN)
formulas.notes: The law states that as the sample size increases, the sample mean will converge to the population mean.
formulas.formula: As the sample size increases, the sample mean will get closer to the population mean
formulas.variables: Sample mean, Population mean, Sample size
